cluster.title: Environmental Stressors — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: UV, CO₂, heat, cold — four atmospheric loads that degrade performance.
cluster.intro: Four instruments for the external loads on a human chassis: UV burn time by skin type, indoor CO₂ vs cognitive performance, WBGT heat-stress index, and required clothing insulation in clo units.
cluster.description: UV burn-time by Fitzpatrick type and SPF, CO₂ cognitive-decline mapping, WBGT heat-stress index, and clothing clo calculator.

faq.q: Why WBGT instead of heat index?
faq.a: WBGT adds radiant load (globe temp), so it handles direct sun. Heat index is dry + humidity only. WBGT is the OSHA and ACGIH standard.
The absolute thermoregulatory limit — evaporation can no longer cool a healthy adult. Fatal within hours regardless of hydration or fitness.

tools.title: UV Exposure Delta — Burn Time Calculator | Kefiw

current.description: Minutes to a Minimum Erythemal Threshold by Fitzpatrick skin type and UV Index. Protected time via SPF.
current.intro: Minimum Erythemal Threshold (MED) varies by Fitzpatrick type — type I burns at 200 J/m², type VI at 1000 J/m². At UV Index 1 ≈ 25 mW/m² erythemally weighted, burn_min = MED / (UVI × 1.5).
current.outcomeLine: Burn time by Fitzpatrick skin type, UV index, and applied SPF.
howTo: Enter current UV index (from weather), pick skin type, enter SPF applied.
Read unprotected burn time and the SPF-multiplied protected window.

examples.title: UV 7 · Type II · SPF 30
examples.body: Unprotected 24 min to MED · protected 12 h — if you reapply every 2 h.

faq.q: Does SPF really multiply burn time?
faq.a: In lab conditions (2 mg/cm² application). Real-world application delivers ¼ to ½ of labeled SPF. Reapply every 2 h.
MED resets overnight. Melanin buildup (tanning) does push MED slightly over weeks — but at the cost of DNA damage.

tools.title: CO₂ Cognitive Tax — Indoor Air Calculator | Kefiw

current.description: Indoor CO₂ ppm to cognitive decline mapping. 1400 ppm halves strategic decision-making performance.
current.intro: Satish 2012 and Allen 2016 established an exposure-response: at 1000 ppm, strategic thinking drops ~15%; at 1400 ppm, 50%; at 2500, 75%. ASHRAE indoor target: ≤ 1000 ppm. Most offices and bedrooms hit 1200–2500 without mechanical ventilation.
current.outcomeLine: Indoor CO₂ ppm mapped to cognitive decline — vent or vacate above 1500.
howTo: Read CO₂ from a monitor (outdoor baseline ~420 ppm).
Read cognitive decline, status band, and action.
Open a window or vacate above 1500 ppm for sustained cognitive work.

faq.a: Mechanism still debated — likely cerebral vasoconstriction and acid-base shift, not hypoxia. Effect reverses within minutes of ventilation.
420 ppm globally, rising ~2 ppm/year. Indoor ppm = outdoor + occupancy load ÷ ventilation rate.

tools.title: Thermal Failure — WBGT Heat Stress Calculator | Kefiw

current.description: Wet-bulb globe temperature — the heat-stress index used by OSHA, ACGIH, and military for work/rest cycle decisions.
current.intro: Heat kills through evaporative failure, not raw temperature. WBGT = 0.7·Tw + 0.2·Tg + 0.1·Td (outdoor) captures humidity (wet-bulb), radiant load (globe), and air temp (dry). Wet-bulb > 35°C = fundamental limit of human thermoregulation.
current.outcomeLine: WBGT heat-stress index — the ACGIH work/rest cycle standard.
howTo: Enter wet-bulb, globe, and dry-bulb temps in °F or °C.
Read WBGT and the ACGIH status band (nominal / moderate / high / extreme / fatal-risk).

examples.title: Tw 75 · Tg 85 · Td 88 · sun
examples.body: WBGT 26.1 °C · Nominal. Moderate band begins at 27 °C.

faq.q: Why wet-bulb and not heat index?
faq.a: WBGT handles radiant and direct-sun load; heat index is dry + humidity only. WBGT is the ISO 7243 and ACGIH TLV standard.
Evaporative cooling fails — skin can't shed heat faster than metabolism generates it. Fatal within hours regardless of hydration, shade, or fitness.

tools.title: Insulation Logic — Clothing Clo Calculator | Kefiw

current.description: Required clothing insulation in clo units for air temperature and activity level. Suggested garment stack.
current.intro: Clo = (33°C − Ta) / (8 × met). 1 clo ≈ business suit. Higher activity generates more metabolic heat, lowering required insulation. Cold under-dressed bodies divert bio-fuel from muscle to thermogenesis — pairs with Kinetic Expenditure.
current.outcomeLine: Required clo for air temp + activity; greedy garment-stack suggestion.
howTo: Enter air temperature and activity state.
Read required clo; the suggested garment stack greedy-packs layers to meet the target.

examples.title: 40 °F · brisk hike
examples.body: Required 0.7 clo · stack: fleece mid-layer + thermal base.

faq.a: 0.155 m²·K/W — the insulation of a 1950s business suit at rest in 21 °C, 50% RH, no wind.

faq.q: Why lower clo at higher activity?
faq.a: Metabolic heat production rises with met, so the body needs less external insulation. Runners over-dress and dump heat.

enhancement_brief.goal: Lift each article from short-form summary to authority-grade long-form. Target 1,200+ words per article while keeping the structured fields crisp.
per_article_outputs: 1. Fill `enhanced` with any structured-field overrides (usually: tighter intro, expanded keyPoints, more examples, richer FAQ).
2. Fill `longform_markdown` with 800-1200 word prose body (markdown, H2 sections only — no H1).
3. Do not modify `current` — that is the baseline snapshot for diffing.

rules: Each question must read like a real search query — not marketing-speak.
First sentence of answer is a complete standalone 15-25 word definition/answer (Google quotes it directly).
Full answer 40-60 words. Tight. Front-load the answer.
Cover distinct intents: definition, comparison, edge-case, how-to, troubleshooting. Not 3 rewordings of the same angle.
Include one target keyword in at least half the questions.
Add a faq_intent tag per entry: "definition" | "comparison" | "edge-case" | "how-to" | "troubleshooting" | "cost" | "trust"
faq_rules.target_count: 5-7 entries per article, 4-6 per tool page

structure: ## H2 section 1 — deeper "what is it" / definition with context
## H2 section 2 — the mechanic / how it works (with one small example)
## H2 section 3 — when to use vs when to avoid
## H2 section 4 — worked example or walkthrough
## H2 section 6 — tool-specific tips (link out to the tool)
longform_rules.tone: Concrete, useful, first-person-plural light. No filler. No "in conclusion". Short paragraphs.
links: Every time you mention a sibling tool in this cluster, link it with the exact URL from this brief (tools[].url).
Every time you mention a sibling article, link it with the exact URL from this brief (articles[].url).
Do not invent URLs or link to external competitors.
forbidden: Medical/legal/financial advice language ("you should", "we recommend") — use neutral framing.
Generic SEO boilerplate ("in today's fast-paced world").

internal_linking.rule: Use only URLs that appear in this JSON. Prefer linking to sibling tools/articles in THIS cluster over outbound links.

quality_gates: No fabricated facts — if uncertain, phrase as "in practice" or remove.
Every H2 section earns its space — cut if it restates the intro.
Zero AI-tells: "it is important to note", "in conclusion", "let us delve", "furthermore".
